does: This paper proves that prompt injection attacks represent a fundamental architectural vulnerability in shared-embedding language models rather than a solvable engineering problem. The authors formalize prompted systems as Prompted Action Models that generate control-authoritative outputs including refusal decisions, tool authorization, and policy routing. They introduce Semantic-Faithful Control (SFC) as a security property requiring that such behaviors depend only on input meaning, not encoding. Through three mathematical results, they prove SFC is provably unachievable in shared-pipeline architectures: they demonstrate that shared representations make trusted and untrusted content statistically inseparable via provenance-recovery impossibility bounds, show that untrusted tokens necessarily flow into control-relevant computation through the same attention mechanisms that produce outputs, and identify gaps between finite training coverage and universal invariance guarantees.
connects: This work builds on the recent proliferation of prompt-injection attacks and proposed defenses that have repeatedly failed despite engineering sophistication. It extends the formal adversarial ML literature that analyzes fundamental limits of security in learning systems, particularly work examining whether training-based approaches can guarantee robustness properties. The paper connects to architecture-level security discussions in deep learning, drawing parallels to known limitations in other domains where instruction and data share representations. It also relates to research on attention mechanisms, token embedding spaces, and the information flow through transformer layers that prior work has begun to understand mechanistically.
enables: This negative result motivates architectural redesigns separating instruction and data processing in language models. The formalization of Prompted Action Models and Semantic-Faithful Control provides a framework for evaluating alternative architectures that enforce control-data boundaries at design time rather than relying on learned distinctions. The impossibility proofs suggest future work should explore: explicitly gated architectures where trusted code operates on untrusted data through restricted interfaces, hardware-enforced separation analogous to systems security, and formal methods for verifying control integrity. This work also opens questions about whether hybrid approaches combining multiple models, external policy engines, or fundamentally different paradigms beyond shared embeddings could achieve meaningful security guarantees for LLM-integrated applications.
